questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: What are some of the themes covered in the Psalms?
questions.answer: Themes covered in the Psalms include: prophecies of the coming Messiah, prayers for the king, personal lamentations, songs of praise and hymns for special feasts. These books also foretell coming events, recall history, frame laws of life, and reveal what must be done to obey God's word.
questions.explanation: See chapter 3: Old Testament 2: Wisdom & Prophecy
questions.source_excerpt: Themes covered in the Psalms include: prophecies of the coming Messiah, prayers for the king, personal lamentations, songs of praise and hymns for special feasts. These books also foretell coming events, recall history, frame laws of life, and reveal what must be done to obey God's word.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: Who among the major prophets described the new covenant?
questions.answer: Prophet Jeremiah foretells the capture of Jews by the Babylonians and destruction of the temple in Jerusalem. He also prophesied the 'new covenant' between God and mankind to be fulfilled through the Messiah of Israel.
questions.explanation: See chapter 3: Old Testament 2: Wisdom & Prophecy
questions.source_excerpt: The major prophets include Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel and Daniel. The Septuagint represents the first major effort in translating a significant religious text from one language into another.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: What challenges and insecurities did Moses face?
questions.answer: Moses faced many challenges: finding water in the Shur Desert (the water at Marah was bitter to drink), the Israelites grumbling about having no bread and meat, maintaining the faith of the people in God, setting rules and solving altercations, and the Israelites worshiping the golden calf. When God intended to destroy the Israelites, Moses interceded and prayed for mercy. His experiences were anything but pleasant, but he continued to follow God's instructions.
questions.explanation: See chapter 4: Moses – God's Chosen Leader
questions.source_excerpt: It didn't take very long for God to give Moses and the Israelites some challenges that would stretch their faith and patience. Moses led the Israelites through the Shur Desert for three days before they could find water.

questions.section: Synthesized
questions.question: What does Hebrews 11:23 say?
questions.answer: "By faith Moses’ parents hid him for three months after he was born, because they saw he was no ordinary child, and they were not afraid of the king's edict." (Hebrews 11:23)
questions.explanation: Synthesized from chapter 4: Moses – God's Chosen Leader. Answer cited directly from text. Page reference corrected during review.
questions.source_excerpt: Hebrews 11:23 says, “By faith Moses’ parents hid him for three months after he was born, because they saw he was no ordinary child, and they were not afraid of the king's edict.” He faced death as soon as he was born, like our Lord Jesus Christ.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: What are the major divisions of the New Testament?
questions.answer: The New Testament is divided into three sections: 1) Gospels & Acts (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, and Acts of the Apostles), 2) Epistles (Pauline Epistles: Romans to Hebrews; General/Catholic Epistles: James to Jude), and 3) Apocalyptic Text (Revelation).
questions.explanation: See chapter 5: New Testament I: A New Covenant
questions.source_excerpt: The New Testament is divided as follows: Gospels & Acts (Matthew, Mark, Luke and John; Acts), Epistles (Pauline: Romans to Hebrews; General: James to Jude), and Apocalyptic Text (Revelation).

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: What is the Gospel?
questions.answer: The Gospel consists of the four books — Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John — which detail the life and teachings of Jesus Christ. The New Testament is a compilation of 27 books and letters written at various times in Greek, the common language of the time.
questions.explanation: See chapter 5: New Testament I: A New Covenant
questions.source_excerpt: The Gospel consists of the four books — Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John — which detail the life and teachings of Jesus Christ.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: Why do we not read Revelations during the Holy Qurbana?
questions.answer: Revelation can be a difficult text to read because of its rich imagery and symbolism regarding things we may not understand. This is the reason for not including the Book of Revelation in our Church lectionary.
questions.explanation: See chapter 5: New Testament I: A New Covenant
questions.source_excerpt: Revelation can be a difficult text to read because of its rich imagery and symbolism regarding things we may not understand. This is the reason for not including the Book of Revelation in our Church lectionary.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: How can we broadly categorize the epistles of St. Paul?
questions.answer: St. Paul's letters can be broadly categorized into two groups: letters addressed to Christian communities in various places (Romans, 1 & 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 & 2 Thessalonians, with Hebrews written to Jewish converts) and letters addressed to individuals (1 & 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon), which the lesson calls pastoral letters because they were written to leaders of the churches.
questions.explanation: Corrected to match the textbook's two-category structure for Pauline letters.
questions.source_excerpt: Pauline letters can be broadly categorized into two: Addressed to Communities: Nine letters were addressed to Christian communities... Hebrews was written to the Jewish converts. Addressed to Individuals: Four letters were addressed to individuals (I & II Timothy, Titus, Philemon). These letters are also called pastoral letters...

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: List the prophecies and symbols of St. Mary from Scripture.
questions.answer: Scriptural prophecies and symbols of St. Mary include: Genesis 3:15, where the woman's seed refers to Christ conceived by the Holy Spirit; Isaiah 7:14, 'Behold a virgin shall conceive, and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel'; symbols of the unnatural birth such as the ram/sheep in the tree, Aaron's rod that bloomed, Gideon's fleece, and the rock from which water flowed; symbols of her ever-virginity such as the burning bush, the closed gate in Ezekiel 44:2, and the enclosed garden/sealed fountain in Song of Solomon 4:12; Ezekiel's chariot as a symbol of Mary as the human chariot of the Lord; and the Ark of the Covenant and vessel of manna as symbols of Mary bearing Christ, the Law-giver and true bread from heaven.
questions.explanation: Expanded from a partial list to the full list given in the textbook.
questions.source_excerpt: Prophecies and Symbols of Virgin Mary: 'Her seed shall bruise thy head' (Genesis 3:15)... 'Behold a virgin shall conceive...' (Isaiah 7:14)... the rod of Aaron... the fleece of Gideon... the rock... The angel of the Lord appeared... in flames of fire from within a bush... This gate is to remain shut... You are a garden locked up... The Arc of Covenant... and the vessel that kept manna symbolizes Virgin Mary.

questions.section: Synthesized
questions.question: What happened In 394 AD?
questions.answer: In 394 AD, the Holy Girdle of St. Mary, together with the coffin of St. Thomas, was moved from Mylapore to Raha and established in the Church later known as the Church of the Holy Girdle.
questions.explanation: Synthesized from chapter 7: St. Mary – The Handmaid of God. Answer cited directly from text. Page reference corrected during review.
questions.source_excerpt: In 394 AD, together with the coffin of St. Thomas, the Holy Girdle of St. Mary was moved from Mylapore to Raha and established in the Church later known as the Church of the Holy Girdle.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: Describe a) Old Testament Traditions, b) New Testament Traditions and c) Patristic Traditions
questions.answer: a) Old Testament traditions were handed down before Scripture was recorded: earlier generations verbally preserved belief in one God, historical events, and laws until Moses, who was divinely inspired to write them and receive the Ten Commandments. b) New Testament traditions include divine tradition taught by Jesus directly to the Apostles and apostolic tradition transmitted by the Apostles to their successors, including teachings, sacraments, enacted laws, liturgies, and the Gospel first preached orally. c) Patristic traditions are what the Church received from the Holy Fathers, including interpretation of doctrines, exegesis of Scripture, religious rites, enacted laws, synod decisions, and works of the Fathers.
questions.explanation: Replaced an incomplete answer with separate Old Testament, New Testament, and Patristic tradition descriptions.
questions.source_excerpt: Old Testament Tradition... predecessors of several generations handed down the belief in one God... New Testament Tradition... Divine tradition is that teaching given by Jesus Christ directly... Apostolic tradition is the teachings of Christ which was orally communicated... Patristic tradition is what the Holy Church has received... from its Holy Fathers.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: What authority did Jesus give to his disciples in the New Testament?
questions.answer: In the New Testament, Jesus told His disciples: 'Heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, raise the dead, cast out demons. Freely you have received, freely give' (Matthew 10:8). The priesthood continues Christ's ministry of healing, teaching, and administering the sacraments.
questions.explanation: See chapter 9: Holy Priesthood
questions.source_excerpt: In the New Testament, Jesus told His disciples: 'Heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, raise the dead, cast out demons. Freely you have received, freely give' (Matthew 10:8). The priesthood continues Christ's ministry of healing, teaching, and administering the sacraments.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: What are the prerequisites for a couple to be married in the Orthodox Church?
questions.answer: Prerequisites for marriage in the Syriac Orthodox Church include: the marriage should be officiated by canonically ordained clergy, for one man and one woman who obtained valid Holy Baptism and Holy Chrism. The couple must not be closely related by blood or spiritual kinship.
questions.explanation: See chapter 10: Holy Matrimony
questions.source_excerpt: Prerequisites for marriage in the Syriac Orthodox Church include: the marriage should be officiated by canonically ordained clergy, for one man and one woman who obtained valid Holy Baptism and Holy Chrism. The couple must not be closely related by blood or spiritual kinship.

questions.section: Synthesized
questions.question: What is Matrimony?
questions.answer: Matrimony is defined as the sacrament by which a baptized man and a baptized woman bind themselves for life in a lawful marriage and receive the grace to discharge their duties.
questions.explanation: Synthesized from chapter 10: Holy Matrimony. Answer cited directly from text.
questions.source_excerpt: Matrimony is defined as the sacrament by which a baptized man and a baptized woman bind themselves for life in a lawful marriage and receive the grace to discharge their duties.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: Explain how the holy sacraments affect your personal salvation. Use one sacrament as an example to illustrate your answer.
questions.answer: The sacraments affect personal salvation because salvation is a lifelong process of cooperation with God, and the sacraments make us receptive to God's grace and unite us more closely to Christ and His Church. For example, Holy Baptism is the sacrament of entry into the Church, the body of Christ, and the first step toward salvation; through baptism we join in Christ's death, burial, and resurrection and begin the born-again life. Holy Eucharist is another example: Christ taught that unless we eat His flesh and drink His blood, we have no life in us, and through Holy Communion we abide in Him and receive spiritual nourishment for eternal life.
questions.explanation: Added the required sacrament example instead of only stating the general doctrine.
questions.source_excerpt: Sacraments make us receptive to God... By participating in the Sacraments, we grow closer to God and receive our ultimate salvation and eternal life... Holy Baptism is the sacrament of entry into the Church... Jesus taught His followers the necessity of receiving His Body and Blood in order to achieve salvation.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: Why is salvation an ongoing process and not limited to a one-time event?
questions.answer: The Church teaches that our personal salvation is neither a gift, nor a simple work, but rather a progressive process and is realized with the cooperation of God and Man. The life-long processes to achieve salvation outlined here reflect the mutual action and commitment of God and man.
questions.explanation: See chapter 11: Salvation
questions.source_excerpt: The Church teaches that our personal salvation is neither a gift, nor a simple work, but rather a progressive process and is realized with the cooperation of God and Man. The life-long processes to achieve salvation outlined here reflect the mutual action and commitment of God and man.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: What is meant by physical death and resurrection of body?
questions.answer: Physical death means the separation of the soul from the body by the order of God, after which the body returns to dust. The resurrection of the body means that, at the second coming of Christ, the body will be raised, transformed into an incorruptible spiritual body, and reunited with the soul for final judgment.
questions.explanation: See chapter 12: Eschatology: An Orthodox Perspective
questions.source_excerpt: Physical death is the separation of the soul from the body by the order of God. The body returns to dust, but at the second coming the body will be resurrected, transformed, and reunited with the soul for final judgment.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: What is the state of a righteous soul?
questions.answer: The righteous soul remains alive and conscious after death. The lesson says righteous souls are alive, can see, can hear, and can talk; it gives examples such as God being the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, Abraham rejoicing to see Christ's day, the dead hearing the voice of the Son of God, and Moses and Elijah speaking with Jesus at the Transfiguration. The Church also believes the state of souls can be affected by the love and prayers of the righteous until the Last Judgment.
questions.explanation: Cleaned up an incomplete copied excerpt into a direct answer.
questions.source_excerpt: The State of Righteous Soul: The souls are alive... The souls can see... The soul can hear... The soul can talk... Our Church believes that the state of the souls can be affected by the love and prayers of the righteous up until the Last Judgment.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: Briefly explain the signs of the second coming.
questions.answer: The lesson lists six signs or events before the Second Coming: the preaching of the Gospel to all nations; the return of Israel to Christ; the coming of two witnesses; the appearance of the Anti-Christ; a massive falling away from faith led by false prophets with powerful signs and wonders; and wars, revolutions, famine, and natural disasters.
questions.explanation: Corrected the list to the six signs actually listed in the textbook.
questions.source_excerpt: Scripture mentions the following six events that will occur before the second coming of Jesus: The preaching of the Gospel to all nations... The return of Israel to Christ... Coming of two witnesses... Appearance of Anti-Christ... A massive falling away from faith... Wars, revolutions, famine and natural disasters.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: Describe the early transition of Asceticism to Christian Monasticism?
questions.answer: Christian Monasticism did not originate as an order within the Church. The primary cause for the rise of Monasticism was the laxness in the life of Christians after Christianity was declared legal and persecution ended. Many people believed that asceticism and detachment from mainstream society was the best way to serve Jesus. Some zealous Christians moved to the deserts to practice asceticism. Thus, individual asceticism became the first form of Monasticism.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: What is the role of monasticism in strengthening the faith of the Church and the faithful?
questions.answer: When the Church faced hard times because of rulers or heretics, the monks abandoned their caves and went into the cities to help the faithful bear the oppression and to strengthen them in faith with their true teachings. The faithful and society considered the monks as the true bearers of the teachings of Jesus. The Church has recognized monastic life and has chosen bishops and sometimes the patriarchs from among the monks.

questions.answer: According to the lesson, Protestant influences in India opposed important practices and teachings of the Syriac Orthodox Church. The text says Anglican/CMS Protestant teaching pushed against the intercession of saints, devotion to St. Mary and the saints, prayers for the departed, and the Church's legitimate apostolic priesthood. It also notes that the Church of South India uses a liturgy formed from Anglican, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Congregational sources, while the Mar Thoma Syrian Church disowns St. Mary and the saints and does not have prayers for the departed.

They disown St. Mary and all other saints and they do not have prayers for departed.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: How can one describe the transition of New Testament spirituality through its various stages?
questions.answer: New Testament spirituality moved through several stages. In the early Church it was eschatological, centered on expectation of Christ's return, sharing possessions, evangelistic work, breaking bread, and secret prayers during persecution. During persecution, martyrdom became a major expression of spirituality. After persecutions lessened, asceticism, celibacy, and monasticism developed as important forms of spiritual life. Later, from around the 16th century, spirituality also developed in nation-based forms.
questions.explanation: See chapter 15: Foundations of Orthodox Spirituality
questions.source_excerpt: New Testament spirituality began with an eschatological expectation of Christ's return, sharing wealth, evangelism, breaking bread, and secret prayers during persecution. It later emphasized martyrdom, then asceticism, celibacy and monasticism after persecutions lessened, and later nation-based spirituality.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: How do you describe genuine fasting that pleases God?
questions.answer: Genuine fasting that pleases God is not merely dieting or self-punishment. It includes repentance, prayer, abstaining from unnecessary entertainment and worldly pleasures, controlling what comes out of the mouth, and practicing almsgiving by helping the needy.
questions.explanation: See chapter 16: Spirituality in Practice
questions.source_excerpt: Genuine fasting is not merely dieting or self-punishment. It includes repentance, prayer, abstaining from unnecessary entertainment and worldly pleasures, controlling what comes out of the mouth, and giving to the needy.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: Consider tracking and how much you spend on a daily, monthly and yearly basis and what you spend it on. Is there a way to redirect your spending towards the Church and charity?
questions.answer: Yes. A Christian should examine spending habits and redirect excess or unnecessary spending toward the Church and charity. The lesson gives this ideal money principle: work hard to make money, spend conservatively, give to charity, save for the future, and be rich in faith.
questions.explanation: See chapter 18: Christian Financial Stewardship
questions.source_excerpt: An ideal target for money principle can be said as this: work hard to make money, spend conservatively, give to charity, save for the future and be rich in faith.

questions.section: Checkpoint
questions.question: When you have excess what should you do? Why?
questions.answer: When you have excess, you should avoid waste and use what remains to help others, especially the poor and needy. The lesson cites John 6:12, where Jesus tells the disciples to gather leftover fragments so nothing is lost, and also points to Jesus' instruction to the rich man to give to the poor and have treasure in heaven. The reason is that reducing waste and sharing with others are part of faithful Christian stewardship.
questions.explanation: Turned copied source text into a complete answer to 'what should you do and why?'.
questions.source_excerpt: John 6:12 writes: 'And when they had eaten their fill, he told his disciples, Gather up the fragments left over, that nothing may be lost.' Reducing waste should be a Christian's goal. Jesus tells the rich man: 'Sell everything you have and give to the poor, and you will have treasure in heaven.'

questions.question: What are the Books of the Law in the Septuagint?
questions.answer: The Books of the Law are Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy. The textbook also calls these first five books the Pentateuch or Torah, the Holy Book of instruction/law, and says they describe God's creation and continue through the days of Moses.

questions.section: Opening Activities
questions.question: What are the four sections of the Septuagint?
questions.answer: The four sections of the Septuagint are: 1) Books of the Law: Genesis to Deuteronomy; 2) Books of Israel's History: Joshua to Maccabees; 3) Books of Wisdom: Psalms to Wisdom of Sirach; and 4) Books of Prophecy: Hosea to Daniel.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What was a key aspect of Marcion of Sinope's heresy?
options: He rejected all four Gospels entirely and relied only on the Epistles of St. Paul
He added extra books to the New Testament canon from pagan sources
He accepted the Old Testament but rejected all New Testament writings as fraudulent
He rejected the Old Testament and accepted only an edited version of St. Luke's Gospel
questions.answer: He rejected the Old Testament and accepted only an edited version of St. Luke's Gospel
questions.explanation: Marcion completely rejected the OT, rejected all Gospels except St. Luke, and edited St. Luke by removing all connections of Jesus to the Old Testament.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: When did Marcion of Sinope make his canon, considered the first attempt to canonize the New Testament?
options: 325 AD
questions.explanation: Marcion made a canon which is considered to be the first attempt to canonize the New Testament around 130-140 AD.

Very Rev. Kurian Arch Corepiscopos
Konattu Very Rev. Mathan Corepiscopa
Kayamkulam Philipose Ramban
questions.answer: Kayamkulam Philipose Ramban
questions.explanation: The translations of the Bible into Malayalam began from 1806, and the first attempt was done by Kayamkulam Philipose Ramban, who translated four Gospels from Syriac to Malayalam (printed 1811).

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What is the official Bible of the Syriac Orthodox Church, and who translated it?
options: 'Ramban Bible' — translated by Kayamkulam Philipose Ramban from Greek to Malayalam in 1811
'Peshita Malabar' — translated by Benjamin Bailey from Latin to Malayalam in 1850
'Vishudha Grandham' — translated by Very Rev. Kurian Arch Corepiscopos from Syriac to Malayalam in 1994
'Syriac Grandham' — translated by Konattu Mathan Corepiscopa from Hebrew to Malayalam in 1936
questions.answer: 'Vishudha Grandham' — translated by Very Rev. Kurian Arch Corepiscopos from Syriac to Malayalam in 1994
questions.explanation: In 1994, Very Rev. Kurian Arch Corepiscopos translated the whole Peshita Bible from Syriac to Malayalam. Published as 'Vishudha Grandham', this is the official Bible of our Church.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What did Marcion believe about Jesus' body?
options: Jesus had a fully human body identical to all other humans with no divine nature
Jesus' body was created from heavenly matter different from earthly substance
Jesus never had a physical body and only appeared as a spirit to the Apostles
Jesus' body was only an imitation of a material body, denying His physical birth, death, and resurrection
questions.answer: Jesus' body was only an imitation of a material body, denying His physical birth, death, and resurrection
questions.explanation: Marcion believed that Jesus' body was only an imitation of a material body, denying Jesus' physical birth, death and resurrection.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What does the word 'Genesis' mean?
options: Law
Creation
Beginning
Covenant
questions.answer: Beginning
questions.explanation: 'Genesis', meaning 'beginning', describes God's creation of the world and the fall of man beginning with Adam and Eve.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which three women are featured in the Septuagint as examples of courage and faith?
options: Mary, Martha, and Sarah
Hannah, Miriam, and Rebekah
Ruth, Esther, and Judith
Deborah, Rachel, and Leah
questions.answer: Ruth, Esther, and Judith
questions.explanation: Ruth, Esther, and Judith are featured in the Septuagint as women who showed courage and faith.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What do Chapters 1-11 of Genesis describe?
options: The history of man from creation, Noah and the flood, and the scattering of nations from the Tower of Babel
The laws given to Moses at Mount Sinai and the covenant with Israel
The history of Abraham and his family leading to the twelve tribes of Israel
The journey of the Israelites through the wilderness to the Promised Land
questions.answer: The history of man from creation, Noah and the flood, and the scattering of nations from the Tower of Babel
questions.explanation: The first part of Genesis (Chapters 1-11) tells about the history of man from creation, Noah and the flood, and the scattering of nations from the Tower of Babel. Chapters 12-50 contain the history of Abraham and his family.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which major prophet foretold the 'new covenant' between God and mankind?
options: Ezekiel
Daniel
Jeremiah
Isaiah
questions.answer: Jeremiah
questions.explanation: Prophet Jeremiah foretells the capture of Jews by the Babylonians and prophesied the 'new covenant' between God and mankind to be fulfilled through the Messiah of Israel.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Why is the book of Isaiah referred to as the 'fifth gospel'?
options: Because Isaiah contains five major sections corresponding to the five books of Moses
Because Isaiah was written after the four canonical Gospels as a supplement
Because Isaiah was the fifth book added to the Septuagint canon
Because Isaiah made many detailed prophecies about the coming of the Messiah, including the virgin birth, Christ's suffering, resurrection, and second coming
questions.answer: Because Isaiah made many detailed prophecies about the coming of the Messiah, including the virgin birth, Christ's suffering, resurrection, and second coming
questions.explanation: Prophet Isaiah writes about his visions wherein the coming of Messiah is described in great detail — the virgin birth, Christ's suffering, resurrection, and second coming. Due to the many prophecies he made, the book is referred to as the fifth gospel.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Who are the four major prophets of the Old Testament?
options: Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Daniel, and Amos
Moses, Isaiah, Jeremiah, and Elijah
Isaiah, Elijah, Ezekiel, and Daniel
Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Daniel
questions.answer: Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, and Daniel
questions.explanation: The major prophets include Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel and Daniel.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a theme covered in the Psalms?
options: Personal lamentations and prayers for the king
Prophecies of the coming Messiah
Detailed genealogies of the twelve tribes of Israel
Songs of praise and hymns for special feasts
questions.answer: Detailed genealogies of the twelve tribes of Israel
questions.explanation: Themes covered in the Psalms include: prophecies of the coming Messiah, prayers for the king, personal lamentations, songs of praise and hymns for special feasts. They also foretell coming events, recall history, frame laws of life, and reveal what must be done to obey God's word.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: How were the 120 years of Moses' life divided into three phases?
options: 40 years in Egypt, 40 years in Canaan, 40 years leading Israel through the wilderness
50 years in Egypt, 30 years in Midian, 40 years leading Israel through the wilderness
30 years in Egypt, 50 years in Midian, 40 years leading Israel through the wilderness
40 years in Egypt, 40 years in Midian, 40 years leading Israel through the wilderness
questions.answer: 40 years in Egypt, 40 years in Midian, 40 years leading Israel through the wilderness
questions.explanation: Moses lived 120 years: 1st forty years in Egypt (1526-1486 B.C.), 2nd forty years in Midian (1486-1446 B.C.), final forty years leading the children of Israel through the wilderness (1446-1406 B.C.).

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: According to Hebrews 11:23, why did Moses' parents hide him for three months after he was born?
options: Because they saw he was no ordinary child and were not afraid of the king's edict
Because an angel appeared to them in a dream warning them of danger
Because a prophecy foretold that the deliverer would be born that year
Because the midwives told them Pharaoh planned to inspect all Hebrew homes
questions.answer: Because they saw he was no ordinary child and were not afraid of the king's edict
questions.explanation: Hebrews 11:23 says, 'By faith Moses' parents hid him for three months after he was born, because they saw he was no ordinary child, and they were not afraid of the king's edict.'

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: How does Moses' early life foreshadow Jesus Christ?
options: Moses rose from the dead after three days, foreshadowing Jesus' resurrection
Moses' escape from Pharaoh's killing of Hebrew infants parallels Jesus' escape from Herod's slaughter in Bethlehem
Moses was baptized in the Nile, foreshadowing Jesus' baptism in the Jordan
Moses was born in a manger, foreshadowing Jesus' humble birth in Bethlehem
questions.answer: Moses' escape from Pharaoh's killing of Hebrew infants parallels Jesus' escape from Herod's slaughter in Bethlehem
questions.explanation: New Testament writers compared Moses to Jesus: Jesus' escape from Herod's slaughter in Bethlehem is compared to Moses' escape from Pharaoh's designs to kill Hebrew infants.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What are the three major divisions of the New Testament?
options: Law, Poetry, and Prophecy
History, Wisdom, and Revelation
Gospels, Psalms, and Letters
Gospels & Acts, Epistles, and Apocalyptic Text (Revelation)

questions.explanation: The New Testament is divided into: Gospels & Acts (Matthew, Mark, Luke, John; Acts), Epistles (Pauline and General), and Apocalyptic Text (Revelation).

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: How many books make up the New Testament?
questions.explanation: The New Testament is a compilation of 27 books and letters written at various times in Greek, the common language of the time.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Why is the Book of Revelation not included in the Syriac Orthodox Church lectionary?
options: Because it contradicts the teachings found in the Epistles
Because the Church considers it non-canonical and uninspired
Because it was written much later and its authorship is disputed
Because of its rich imagery and symbolism regarding things we may not understand
questions.answer: Because of its rich imagery and symbolism regarding things we may not understand
questions.explanation: Revelation can be a difficult text to read because of its rich imagery and symbolism regarding things we may not understand. This is the reason for not including the Book of Revelation in our Church lectionary.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which set contains letters St. Paul addressed to Christian communities in various places?
options: Romans, 1 & 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, and 1 & 2 Thessalonians
1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon
James, 1 Peter, 2 Peter, and Jude
Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, and Acts

questions.explanation: The lesson says nine letters were addressed to Christian communities in various geographical places: Romans, 1 & 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, and 1 & 2 Thessalonians. Hebrews was written to Jewish converts.
questions.source_excerpt: Addressed to Communities: Nine letters were addressed to Christian communities in various geographical places (Romans, 1 & II Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, I & II Thessalonians). Hebrews was written to the Jewish converts.

options: Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, and Hebrews
1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon
Romans, Galatians, and 1 Corinthians
James, Jude, and Revelation

questions.explanation: The lesson says four letters were addressed to individuals: 1 & 2 Timothy, Titus, and Philemon, and calls these pastoral letters because they were written to leaders of the churches.
questions.source_excerpt: Addressed to Individuals: Four letters were addressed to individuals (I & II Timothy, Titus, Philemon). These letters are also called pastoral letters as they were written to the leaders of the churches.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What does the burning bush in Exodus 3 symbolize in relation to St. Mary?
options: Mary who bore the fire of divinity without being consumed
The Holy Spirit descending on the Apostles at Pentecost
Mary's suffering at the foot of the cross
The purification of Mary through the fire of the Holy Spirit
questions.answer: Mary who bore the fire of divinity without being consumed
questions.explanation: The burning bush (Exodus 3) symbolizes Mary who bore the fire of divinity without being consumed.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What does Genesis 3:15 ('Her seed shall bruise thy head') prophesy about St. Mary?
options: Mary would physically crush a serpent during her lifetime in Nazareth
Mary would be the first woman to enter the Holy of Holies in the Temple
Mary's descendants would become kings who would conquer enemy nations
Mary is the woman whose seed (Christ) would bruise the head of the serpent (defeat evil)

questions.explanation: Genesis 3:15 — 'Her seed shall bruise thy head' — Mary is the woman whose seed would bruise the head of the serpent.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What does Aaron's rod that budded (Numbers 17) symbolize in relation to St. Mary?
options: The priesthood of Christ passed down through Mary's lineage
Mary's role in leading the early Church after the Ascension
The virgin birth — life coming forth miraculously without natural means
Mary's authority as queen of heaven over all the saints
questions.answer: The virgin birth — life coming forth miraculously without natural means
questions.explanation: Aaron's rod that budded (Numbers 17) symbolizes the virgin birth.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What are Apostolic Traditions?
options: Cultural customs adopted from Roman and Greek civilizations by early Christians
Teachings, practices, and beliefs handed down from the Apostles to the Church through successive generations
Only the written texts of the New Testament as compiled by the Apostles
The personal opinions of individual Church leaders throughout history
questions.answer: Teachings, practices, and beliefs handed down from the Apostles to the Church through successive generations
questions.explanation: Apostolic Traditions are the teachings, practices, and beliefs handed down from the Apostles to the Church through successive generations, including liturgical practices, doctrines, and customs not explicitly written in Scripture.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What does Sola Scriptura claim?
options: That Scripture and Tradition together form the complete basis of faith
That each believer must write their own interpretation of Scripture
That the Bible alone is sufficient for all Christian doctrine
That only the Old Testament is necessary for salvation
questions.answer: That the Bible alone is sufficient for all Christian doctrine
questions.explanation: Sola Scriptura claims that the Bible alone is sufficient for all Christian doctrine.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which Bible verse is used to challenge the concept of Sola Scriptura?
options: Romans 10:9 — 'If you confess with your mouth Jesus as Lord, you will be saved'
Genesis 1:1 — 'In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth'
John 3:16 — 'For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son'
2 Thessalonians 2:15 — 'hold to the traditions which you were taught, whether by word of mouth or by our letter'
questions.answer: 2 Thessalonians 2:15 — 'hold to the traditions which you were taught, whether by word of mouth or by our letter'
questions.explanation: Scripture itself does not claim to be the sole authority — 2 Thessalonians 2:15 says 'hold to the traditions which you were taught, whether by word of mouth or by our letter.'

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which statement about the Nicene Creed is true?
options: The Nicene Creed was written 200 years after the New Testament was compiled
The Nicene Creed was adopted from a pre-existing Jewish prayer
The Nicene Creed was written prior to the compilation of the New Testament
The Nicene Creed was written by the Apostle Paul as his final epistle
questions.answer: The Nicene Creed was written prior to the compilation of the New Testament
questions.explanation: The Nicene Creed was written prior to the compilation of the New Testament, demonstrating that Tradition preceded the written canon.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: From which Greek word is the word 'priest' derived, and what does it mean?
options: 'Diakonos' meaning 'servant'
'Hiereus' meaning 'sacred one'
'Presbyteros' meaning 'elder'
'Episkopos' meaning 'overseer'
questions.answer: 'Presbyteros' meaning 'elder'
questions.explanation: The word 'priest' is derived from the Greek word 'Presbyteros' meaning 'elder'.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: In the Old Testament, which tribe did God consecrate for priestly duties?
options: The tribe of Levi
The tribe of Judah
The tribe of Benjamin
The tribe of Dan
questions.answer: The tribe of Levi
questions.explanation: In the Old Testament, God consecrated the tribe of Levi for priestly duties.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: According to Matthew 10:8, what authority did Jesus give to His disciples?
options: 'Feed my sheep and tend my lambs, for I am giving you the keys of heaven'
'Heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, raise the dead, cast out demons. Freely you have received, freely give'
'Build my Church upon this rock, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it'
'Go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit'
questions.answer: 'Heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, raise the dead, cast out demons. Freely you have received, freely give'
questions.explanation: Jesus told His disciples: 'Heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, raise the dead, cast out demons. Freely you have received, freely give' (Matthew 10:8).

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: How is Matrimony defined in the Syriac Orthodox Church?
options: The sacrament by which a baptized man and a baptized woman bind themselves for life in a lawful marriage
A personal commitment that does not require Church involvement or clergy
A blessing given by any religious leader to any couple who requests it
A social contract between two families for the purpose of producing children
questions.answer: The sacrament by which a baptized man and a baptized woman bind themselves for life in a lawful marriage
questions.explanation: Matrimony is defined as the sacrament by which a baptized man and a baptized woman bind themselves for life in a lawful marriage.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which of the following is a prerequisite for marriage in the Syriac Orthodox Church?
options: Both parties must have received valid Holy Baptism and Holy Chrism
Both parties must belong to the same parish for at least five years
Both parties must have completed theological seminary training
Both parties must receive written approval from the Patriarch
questions.answer: Both parties must have received valid Holy Baptism and Holy Chrism
questions.explanation: Prerequisites include: the marriage must be officiated by canonically ordained clergy, for one man and one woman who obtained valid Holy Baptism and Holy Chrism.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: How does the Orthodox Church view personal salvation?
options: A one-time gift received at the moment of verbally accepting Christ
Predetermined by God before birth, with no human participation required
A progressive process realized through the cooperation of God and Man
Purely the result of good works and charitable deeds accumulated over a lifetime
questions.answer: A progressive process realized through the cooperation of God and Man
questions.explanation: The Church teaches that our personal salvation is neither a gift, nor a simple work, but rather a progressive process realized with the cooperation of God and Man.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What is 'Baptism of Blood'?
options: A special type of baptism performed using water mixed with consecrated wine
The baptism ceremony held for infants during their first week of life
When a person believes in Jesus and is martyred before receiving the sacrament of baptism
A ritual performed during Great Lent to commemorate Christ's passion
questions.answer: When a person believes in Jesus and is martyred before receiving the sacrament of baptism
questions.explanation: If a person believes in Jesus and is martyred before his baptism, it is considered as 'Baptism of Blood.'

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What is 'Baptism of Desire'?
options: When a person desires to join the Church but is waiting for the next baptism ceremony
When parents express the desire for their child to be baptized in the future
When a catechumen completes all preparatory classes but has not yet been immersed
When a person believes in Christ and wanted to be baptized but died before receiving it
questions.answer: When a person believes in Christ and wanted to be baptized but died before receiving it
questions.explanation: If a person believes in Christ and wanted to be baptized but died before it, it can be called 'Baptism of desire.'

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What does the term 'eschatology' refer to?
options: The study of Old Testament prophets and their written works
The study of events such as death, end of the world, resurrection of the dead, Second Coming, judgment, heaven and hell
The study of Church governance, hierarchy, and canon law
The study of early Christian liturgical practices and worship
questions.answer: The study of events such as death, end of the world, resurrection of the dead, Second Coming, judgment, heaven and hell
questions.explanation: Eschatology covers the study of events such as death, end of the world, resurrection of the dead, Second Coming of Jesus, judgment, heaven and hell.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: According to Scripture, which of the following is one of the six signs before Christ's second coming?
options: False prophets and false Christs will arise
The physical rebuilding of the Tower of Babel
A worldwide conversion of all nations to Christianity
The visible return of Moses and Elijah to preach in Jerusalem
questions.answer: False prophets and false Christs will arise
questions.explanation: The lesson lists several signs before the Second Coming, including a massive falling away from faith led by false prophets with powerful signs and wonders.
questions.source_excerpt: A massive falling away from faith led by false prophets with powerful signs and wonders.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: How many events does Scripture mention will occur before the second coming of Jesus?
options: Three
Six
Twelve
Ten
questions.answer: Six
questions.explanation: Scripture mentions six events that will occur before the second coming of Jesus.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What does the Syriac Orthodox Church believe about the state of righteous souls after death?
options: The souls cease to exist until they are recreated at the Second Coming
The souls enter a permanent state of unconscious sleep until the resurrection
The souls are immediately judged and assigned to heaven or hell with no further change possible
The souls are alive and their state can be affected by the love and prayers of the righteous until the Last Judgment
questions.answer: The souls are alive and their state can be affected by the love and prayers of the righteous until the Last Judgment
questions.explanation: Our Church believes that the state of the souls can be affected by the love and prayers of the righteous up until the Last Judgment.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What was the primary cause for the rise of Christian monasticism?
options: The discovery of ancient scrolls requiring isolated communities to study and preserve them
A direct commandment from the Apostles to establish monasteries throughout the empire
The laxness in the life of Christians after Christianity was declared legal and persecution ended
The need to hide from intensifying Roman persecution in remote desert caves
questions.answer: The laxness in the life of Christians after Christianity was declared legal and persecution ended
questions.explanation: The primary cause for the rise of Monasticism was the laxness in the life of Christians after Christianity was declared legal and persecution ended.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What are the three monastic vows?
options: Poverty, silence, and daily pilgrimage
Prayer, fasting, and almsgiving
Absolute obedience, voluntary poverty, and chastity
Obedience, public preaching, and community service
questions.answer: Absolute obedience, voluntary poverty, and chastity
questions.explanation: The three monastic vows are: 1) Absolute obedience to his spiritual superior, 2) Voluntary poverty, 3) Chastity oath to never marry and to remain chaste.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What role did monks play when the Church faced hard times from rulers or heretics?
options: They fled to other countries and established new independent churches there
They remained isolated in their monasteries and prayed in silence for the persecution to end
They formed armed militias to physically protect the Church buildings from destruction
They abandoned their caves and went into the cities to help the faithful and strengthen them in faith with true teachings
questions.answer: They abandoned their caves and went into the cities to help the faithful and strengthen them in faith with true teachings
questions.explanation: When the Church faced hard times, the monks abandoned their caves and went into the cities to help the faithful and strengthen them in faith with their true teachings.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: In which city were the disciples of Christ first called 'Christians'?
options: Alexandria
Antioch
Rome
Jerusalem
questions.answer: Antioch
questions.explanation: It was in Antioch that the disciples of Christ were called Christians for the first time (Acts 11:26).

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What event led to the disciples scattering and eventually reaching Antioch?
options: The destruction of the Temple in Jerusalem by the Romans
The persecution that arose after Stephen's martyrdom
The Council of Jerusalem's decision to send missionaries abroad
The Roman Emperor's decree expelling all Jews from Palestine
questions.answer: The persecution that arose after Stephen's martyrdom
questions.explanation: When persecution arose after Stephen's martyrdom, the disciples scattered, and it was in Antioch that they were called Christians for the first time.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What language does the Syriac Orthodox Church use in its liturgy?
options: Ancient Hebrew — the language of the Old Testament
Syriac — a dialect of Aramaic, the language spoken by Jesus Christ
Latin — the official language of the early Roman Church
Classical Greek — the language of the New Testament manuscripts
questions.answer: Syriac — a dialect of Aramaic, the language spoken by Jesus Christ
questions.explanation: The Syriac Orthodox Church employs in its liturgy the Syriac language, a dialect of Aramaic — the language spoken by Jesus Christ.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: How many sacraments does the Syriac Orthodox Church practice?
options: Three
Two
Five
Seven
questions.answer: Seven
questions.explanation: The Syriac Orthodox Church observes seven sacraments.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which of the following is a key doctrinal difference between the Syriac Orthodox Church and Protestant Churches?
options: The Syriac Orthodox Church rejects the Old Testament, while Protestants accept it
The Syriac Orthodox Church does not believe in the resurrection, while Protestants do
The Syriac Orthodox Church maintains Apostolic succession and tradition alongside Scripture, while Protestants follow Sola Scriptura
The Syriac Orthodox Church uses only the Gospel of Matthew, while Protestants use all four Gospels
questions.answer: The Syriac Orthodox Church maintains Apostolic succession and tradition alongside Scripture, while Protestants follow Sola Scriptura
questions.explanation: The Syriac Orthodox Church maintains Apostolic succession and tradition alongside Scripture, while Protestants follow 'Sola Scriptura'. The Church also practices seven sacraments while most Protestants recognize only two.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: What is the first and most important aspect of Syriac Orthodox Spirituality listed in the lesson?
options: Monastic-Life Oriented
Holy Trinity Centered
Scripture-Only Based
Individual Prayer Focused
questions.answer: Holy Trinity Centered
questions.explanation: The important aspects of Syriac Orthodox Spirituality begin with being Holy Trinity Centered.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: According to Webster's Dictionary, how is addiction defined?
options: An involuntary medical condition caused exclusively by genetic factors
A temporary phase that all people naturally experience during adolescence
A spiritual punishment sent by God for disobedience to His commandments
A chosen behavior by an individual to obsessively or habitually surrender to something
questions.answer: A chosen behavior by an individual to obsessively or habitually surrender to something
questions.explanation: Webster's Dictionary defines addiction as a chosen behavior by an individual 'to obsessively or habitually surrender to something.'

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which of the following is one of the three recommended ways to conquer addiction?
options: Replacing one addiction with a less harmful addiction
Complete self-isolation from family and friends to avoid triggers
Relying solely on willpower without seeking external help
Developing a spiritual support system with parents, teachers, priests, and mentors
questions.answer: Developing a spiritual support system with parents, teachers, priests, and mentors
questions.explanation: Three ways to conquer addiction are: 1) Prayer, 2) Spiritual Support System — with parents, teachers, priests, and mentors, and 3) Abstinence — staying away from high-risk situations.

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: Which of the following are types of addictions mentioned in the lesson?
options: Only substance abuse involving illegal drugs and alcohol
Only addictions that are clinically diagnosed by a physician
Only behavioral addictions like gambling and shopping
Chemical dependencies (cigarettes, alcohol, drugs) and unhealthy natural highs (food, social networking, internet, video gaming)
questions.answer: Chemical dependencies (cigarettes, alcohol, drugs) and unhealthy natural highs (food, social networking, internet, video gaming)
questions.explanation: Types of addictions include chemical dependencies (cigarettes, alcohol, prescription and illegal drugs) and unhealthy natural highs (food, social networking, internet, video gaming).

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: According to John 6:12, what did Jesus tell His disciples after the feeding of the multitude?
options: 'Gather up the fragments left over, that nothing may be lost'
'Render unto Caesar the things that are Caesar's'
'Store up treasures in heaven where moth and rust cannot destroy'
'Sell everything you have and distribute it to the poor'
questions.answer: 'Gather up the fragments left over, that nothing may be lost'
questions.explanation: John 6:12 writes: 'And when they had eaten their fill, he told his disciples, Gather up the fragments left over, that nothing may be lost.'

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: According to the lesson, what is the ideal principle regarding money?
options: Work hard to make money, spend conservatively, give to charity, save for the future and be rich in faith
Accumulate as much wealth as possible to ensure security for future generations
Avoid working for money altogether and live a purely ascetic lifestyle
Give away all earnings to the Church and trust God to provide daily needs
questions.answer: Work hard to make money, spend conservatively, give to charity, save for the future and be rich in faith
questions.explanation: An ideal target for money principle: 'work hard to make money, spend conservatively, give to charity, save for the future and be rich in faith.'

questions.section: MCQ
questions.question: According to the lesson, when does wealth become 'bad'?
options: When you save money instead of spending it all immediately
When the role of money becomes distorted and takes a high priority in your life
When you earn more than your basic needs require
Wealth is always bad according to Christian teaching
questions.answer: When the role of money becomes distorted and takes a high priority in your life
questions.explanation: Wealth becomes 'bad' when the role of money becomes distorted and takes a high priority in your life.
